summary: Four royal enthronement psalms celebrate God's universal kingship — calling creation to joyful worship, warning against hardened hearts, and proclaiming his holiness and saving righteousness to all nations.
psalm_rows_html:
PsalmTypeAuthorSituationKey ThemeNotable VerseNT Connection
Ps 95Hymn / WarningAnonymous (attrib. David in Heb 4:7)Corporate call to worship; warning against rebellion in the wildernessWorship the Creator-King; do not harden hearts like Meribah/Massah"Come, let us bow down in worship, let us kneel before the LORD our Maker" (v.6)Hebrews 3:7–11; 4:3–11 quotes extensively as a warning against unbelief and exhortation to enter God's rest in Christ
Ps 97Enthronement / HymnAnonymousCelebration of God's reign with theophanic imagery; shaming of idol-worshipersThe LORD reigns in righteousness and justice; fire and nature proclaim his glory; idols are put to shame"The LORD reigns, let the earth be glad; let the distant shores rejoice" (v.1)Hebrews 1:6 cites Ps 97:7 ("let all the angels worship him") in reference to Christ's superiority over angels
Ps 98Hymn / New SongAnonymousPraise for God's past saving acts, revealed to all nations; call for universal and cosmic praiseSing a new song; God's salvation and righteousness made known to the nations; all creation rejoices"Sing to the LORD a new song, for he has done marvelous things" (v.1)Revelation 5:9; 14:3; 15:3 echo the "new song" motif; the worldwide salvation anticipated is fulfilled in the gospel going to all nations
Ps 99Enthronement / Holiness HymnAnonymousCelebration of God's holy kingship; remembrance of Moses, Aaron, and Samuel as intercessorsThe LORD is holy (3x refrain); he answers those who call on him; he forgives yet holds wrongdoers accountable"Exalt the LORD our God and worship at his holy mountain, for the LORD our God is holy" (v.9)The triple "holy" (vv.3,5,9) anticipates Isaiah 6:3 and Revelation 4:8; Christ is the ultimate answer to the intercessory pattern of Moses/Aaron/Samuel (Heb 7:25)
